Cross-Entropy

Quantifying the inefficiency of using a wrong assumption to predict a true distribution.

1. Definitions

Definition: Cross-Entropy

For discrete distributions \(P\) and \(Q\) defined over the same sample space \(\mathcal{X}\), the cross-entropy \(H(P, Q)\) is defined as the expected surprisal of the estimated distribution \(Q\), evaluated under the true distribution \(P\):

\[ H(P, Q) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim P} [ -\log_2 Q(x) ] = -\sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} P(x) \log_2 Q(x) \]

For continuous distributions with probability density functions \(p\) and \(q\), the sum is replaced by an integral: \[ H(P, Q) = -\int_{\mathcal{X}} p(x) \ln q(x) \, dx \]

2. Intuitions & Remarks

The Cost of Being Wrong

In information theory, Cross-Entropy measures the average number of bits needed to identify an event drawn from a true probability distribution \(P\), if a coding scheme is used that is optimized for an estimated (or unnatural) probability distribution \(Q\), rather than the true distribution \(P\).

Intuitively, if you believe the weather follows distribution \(Q\) (e.g., mostly sunny), you will design a communication protocol that assigns very short codes to the message “sunny”. If the actual weather follows distribution \(P\) (e.g., mostly rainy), your protocol will be highly inefficient because you will frequently have to transmit the long, complex codes for “rainy”.

Cross-entropy quantifies this exact inefficiency. It asks: “How many bits on average do I have to transmit if I optimize for \(Q\), but reality actually follows \(P\)?”

3. Formulas & Key Properties

Key Properties of Cross-Entropy

1. Non-Symmetric

Cross-entropy is asymmetric. The cost of assuming \(Q\) when reality is \(P\) is completely different from assuming \(P\) when reality is \(Q\). \[ H(P, Q) \neq H(Q, P) \]

2. Lower Bound (Gibbs’ Inequality)

Cross-entropy is strictly bounded below by the true entropy of the ground truth distribution \(P\). \[ H(P, Q) \ge H(P) \] Equality holds if and only if your estimation perfectly matches reality (\(P = Q\)).

3. Relationship to KL Divergence

Cross-entropy is deeply connected to Shannon Entropy and the Kullback-Leibler (KL) Divergence via a profound decomposition: \[ H(P, Q) = H(P) + D_{KL}(P \| Q) \] This equation states that the total cost of using the wrong distribution \(Q\) is exactly equal to the irreducible, theoretical minimum cost if you knew the true distribution \(P\) (which is \(H(P)\)), plus a “penalty gap” for your estimation error (which is the KL Divergence).

4. Graphs & Visualizations

The relationship expressed by \(H(P, Q) = H(P) + D_{KL}(P \| Q)\) can be visualized through a simple decomposition of the penalty.

5. Relations to Other Fields

Field Interpretation / Mapping Application Example
Deep Learning The most universally used loss function for classification tasks. Training neural networks
Information Theory The length of messages encoded with a suboptimal codebook. Kraft-McMillan inequality

Machine Learning Context: Cross-Entropy Loss

When training a neural network classifier, we are trying to predict the true label distribution \(P\) using the model’s predicted distribution \(Q\). - The true distribution \(P\) for a single sample is typically a one-hot encoded vector (e.g., \([0, 1, 0, 0]\) where the target class has probability 1). - The predicted distribution \(Q\) is the output of the Softmax function (e.g., \([0.1, 0.7, 0.15, 0.05]\)).

If we minimize the Cross-Entropy \(H(P, Q)\), we push \(Q\) to be as close to \(P\) as possible.

Why is \(H(P)\) ignored in practice?

Recall the decomposition \(H(P, Q) = H(P) + D_{KL}(P \| Q)\). In a classification task, the true labels \(P\) are deterministic (one-hot), meaning the true entropy \(H(P)\) is exactly \(0\). Therefore, minimizing Cross-Entropy is mathematically identical to minimizing the KL Divergence between the predictions and the ground truth!

6. Interactive Simulation

Interactive Simulation: Binary Cross-Entropy Loss

Assume the true ground-truth label is $y=1$ (deterministic, $H(P)=0$). Drag the slider to change the model's predicted probability $q$. Watch how the Cross-Entropy loss explodes to infinity as the model becomes highly confident but totally wrong (predicting $q \approx 0$).

q = 0.500
Loss = 1.000 nats

7. Practice Problems

Problem 1: Decomposition of Cross-Entropy

Mathematically derive the decomposition \(H(P, Q) = H(P) + D_{KL}(P \| Q)\) from the base definition of Cross-Entropy. (Hint: Use the definition of KL Divergence \(D_{KL}(P \| Q) = \sum P(x) \log \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}\)).
Reveal Solution to Problem 1
Solution

Starting from the definition of KL Divergence: \[ \begin{aligned} D_{KL}(P \| Q) &= \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} P(x) \log \left( \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)} \right) \\ &= \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} P(x) \big( \log P(x) - \log Q(x) \big) \\ &= \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} P(x) \log P(x) - \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} P(x) \log Q(x) \end{aligned} \]

Notice that the first term is exactly the negative entropy of \(P\), and the second term is the positive Cross-Entropy \(H(P, Q)\): \[ D_{KL}(P \| Q) = -H(P) + H(P, Q) \]

Rearranging the terms, we get the fundamental relationship: \[ H(P, Q) = H(P) + D_{KL}(P \| Q) \]

\(\blacksquare\)

Problem 2: Binary Cross-Entropy Penalty Explosion

For a binary classification task, the Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE) formula simplifies to: \[ \text{BCE} = - \big( y \ln(p) + (1 - y) \ln(1 - p) \big) \] Calculate the BCE loss for a single data point where the true label is \(y=1\) and the model’s predicted probability is \(p=0.001\). Why does the loss become so massive compared to a wrong but less confident prediction (e.g. \(p=0.4\))?
Reveal Solution to Problem 2
Solution

If \(y = 1\), the second term \((1 - y)\) becomes zero, completely eliminating the \(\ln(1-p)\) term. The formula simplifies to: \[ \text{BCE} = -\ln(p) \]

For \(p = 0.4\) (Wrong, but not confidently wrong): \[ \text{BCE} = -\ln(0.4) \approx 0.916 \text{ nats} \]

For \(p = 0.001\) (Highly confident, but completely wrong): \[ \text{BCE} = -\ln(0.001) \approx 6.907 \text{ nats} \]

The loss is over 7 times higher! The logarithmic nature of the loss function massively penalizes a model that is confidently wrong. As the probability \(p\) approaches exactly \(0\) for a true class, the loss approaches positive infinity (\(\lim_{p \to 0} -\ln(p) = \infty\)). This asymmetric explosion forces the model to heavily adjust its weights to never guarantee an incorrect prediction.
